title: Wasted Renders and Memoization

learningObjectives.text: Explain identifying wasted renders using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `memo` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `usememo` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `usecallback` using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Wasted Renders and Memoization is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Performance and Advanced Effects** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Identifying wasted renders** → **`memo`** → **`useMemo`** → **`useCallback`**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Identifying wasted renders
sections.body: A render is wasteful only when measured cost matters and it produces no useful change. Profile realistic interactions before optimizing.

sections.title: `memo`
sections.body: memo can skip a component render when props compare equal. It is an optimization, not a correctness mechanism.

sections.title: `useMemo`
sections.body: useMemo caches a calculation result between renders until dependencies change. Use it only for measured cost or identity-sensitive optimization.

sections.title: `useCallback`
sections.body: useCallback caches a function definition when function identity matters to a consumer or dependency.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Memoization is applied where measured calculation and stable props can skip work.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Wasted Renders and Memoization connects Identifying wasted renders, `memo`, `useMemo`.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which change is the best fix?

questions.explanation: Pass stable primitives, move the constant object outside the component, or memoize it only when the optimization is justified by measurement.

assessmentProfile.label: Code-reading, debugging, and implementation challenge
assessmentProfile.rationale: The topic represents a day-to-day coding skill. The topic quiz must primarily test code reading, debugging, output prediction, or implementation choice.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
